name: Garabandal Prophecy
description: The Garabandal Prophecy refers to a series of alleged apparitions of the Virgin Mary that purportedly occurred between 1961 and 1965 in the rural village of San Sebastián de Garabandal, Spain. Four young girls – Conchita González, Mari Loli Mazón, Jacinta González, and Mari Cruz González – claimed to have experienced numerous visits from the Archangel Michael and the Virgin Mary, who delivered messages concerning the future of humanity. These messages allegedly warned of a coming ‘Warning,’ a ‘Miracle,’ and a ‘Punishment’ if humanity did not repent. Proponents believe these prophecies are genuine divine interventions, pointing to the ecstatic states of the girls during the apparitions, documented testimonies, and alleged miraculous events associated with the site. The 'Warning' is described as a global event where everyone will see their soul as God sees it, understand the consequences of their sins, and be given a final chance to repent. The 'Miracle' is predicted to occur within a year of the Warning, and will purportedly be visible to all in Garabandal. If humanity fails to heed the Warning, a great 'Punishment' is predicted to follow. The Catholic Church has never officially recognized the Garabandal apparitions as authentic. Several investigations have been conducted, and while some clergy members have expressed personal belief in the events, the official position remains cautious. Critics point to inconsistencies in the girls' testimonies over time, the lack of verifiable miracles, and psychological explanations for the phenomena, suggesting that mass hysteria or suggestibility could account for the reported experiences. Mainstream Catholic theologians often emphasize the importance of adhering to Church-approved apparitions and prophecies, viewing unapproved claims with skepticism. The Garabandal prophecies remain a controversial topic, generating both strong devotion and considerable skepticism.
subtitle: Alleged Marian apparitions and prophecies.

claims.claim: The Virgin Mary appeared to four girls in Garabandal.
claims.evidence: Reported ecstatic states of the girls, testimonies from villagers, photographs and films.
claims.criticism: Inconsistencies in testimonies, lack of independent verification, possible psychological explanations (mass hysteria, suggestibility).

claims.claim: A 'Warning' will occur, visible to everyone worldwide.
claims.evidence: Girls' prophecies describe a global event of inner illumination and awareness of sin.
claims.criticism: No such event has occurred since the alleged prophecies, rendering it unverifiable; contradicts the nature of divine intervention.

claims.claim: A 'Miracle' will take place in Garabandal, visible to all.
claims.evidence: Girls' prophecies describe a miracle to occur within a year after the Warning.
claims.criticism: No such miracle has occurred; prophecies have not been fulfilled; lack of empirical evidence.

keyFigures.name: Conchita González
keyFigures.role: One of the four alleged visionaries.

keyFigures.name: Joey Lomangino
keyFigures.role: A key promoter of the Garabandal apparitions.

keyFigures.name: Ramón Pérez
keyFigures.role: Spanish priest initially supportive, later critical

keyFigures.name: Bishop Eugenio Beitia Aldazabal
keyFigures.role: Bishop of Santander, issued negative judgements.

timeline.event: Alleged apparitions of the Virgin Mary in Garabandal.
First official investigation by the Diocese of Santander.
Continued debate and devotion surrounding the Garabandal prophecies.

sources.author: Francisco Sánchez-Ventura y Pascual
sources.work: Las Apariciones de Garabandal: Notas a un Diario
sources.title: Francisco Sánchez-Ventura y Pascual

variants: The Warning, Miracle, and Chastisement of Garabandal
